video_title: John Graham: Inside the Fund Investing for 22 Million Canadians | Podcast | In Good Company
gist: CPP Investments CEO John Graham explains the ~$800B Canadian pension fund's total-portfolio, factor-based approach and signals a currently defensive posture toward US market concentration in a handful of mega-cap tech stocks. He remains constructive on private equity over a 10-15 year horizon despite recent underperformance, defends a no-blanket-divestment policy (including oil & gas, excluding crypto), manages US exposure to a 45-50% band, and views AI so far as a driver of speed and operational leverage rather than better investment decisions.

insights.claim: CPP Investments is willing to forgo some market upside right now to protect the downside, with Graham suggesting the fund may currently be in a period of not fully participating in parts of the market because of concentration risk.
insights.speaker: guest, John Graham (CEO, CPP Investments)
insights.so_what: A major long-horizon allocator flagging that it is deliberately underparticipating due to concentration risk is a defensive signal on crowded US mega-cap exposure; watch for de-risking behavior from large pensions.
insights.quote: there will be times when we are not looking to fully participate in the market in parts of the market where we feel there might be an undue risk of loss

insights.claim: Graham distinguishes a pension plan from a wealth-maximizing vehicle, arguing CPP is not always maximizing return and will trade some upside for downside protection to meet its liability stream.
insights.speaker: guest, John Graham
insights.so_what: Frames why liability-driven investors behave asymmetrically at market extremes; useful lens for anticipating pension flows differing from return-maximizing funds.
insights.quote: we're pension plan, we're not a wealth maximizing vehicle

insights.claim: Graham says public markets, especially the broader indices, are very concentrated into a handful of US-based technology stocks, which is a challenge for most active managers and unlike CPP's own portfolio.
insights.speaker: guest, John Graham
insights.so_what: Confirms the structural headwind for diversified active managers benchmarked against concentrated cap-weighted indices; relevant to active-vs-passive positioning.
insights.quote: the public markets, especially the broader markets, are just very concentrated and very concentrated into a a handful of US-based technology stocks

insights.claim: CPP Investments runs a total portfolio approach built on three decisions in order: how much risk to take, how to diversify across asset classes and geographies, and security selection.
insights.speaker: guest, John Graham
insights.so_what: A replicable decision hierarchy for allocators; risk budgeting precedes asset-class and security choices rather than starting from fixed policy weights.

insights.claim: CPP avoids hard allocations to asset classes and countries, instead thinking in factor space about real economic exposures like duration and inflation sensitivity, because asset-class labels can be misleading and rigid buckets create strange rebalancing behavior.
insights.speaker: guest, John Graham
insights.so_what: Factor-based rather than bucket-based allocation reduces forced rebalancing distortions; a model for large pools managing correlation rather than labels.
insights.quote: We think in factor space. We think about what's the real economic exposure we're bringing into the portfolio

insights.claim: Graham does not view public equity and private equity as different asset classes but as different ownership structures within equities that behave differently through the cycle.
insights.speaker: guest, John Graham
insights.so_what: Challenges the standard allocation practice of treating PE as a distinct diversifier; implies PE is largely equity beta with a different governance wrapper.
insights.quote: I don't view public equity and private equity as as different asset classes. I view them as different kind of ownership structures within equities

insights.claim: Graham frames diversification across asset classes and geographies as fundamentally a risk-management tool rooted in not knowing the future, calling it an act of humility.
insights.speaker: guest, John Graham
insights.so_what: Positions diversification as downside insurance rather than return enhancement; a discipline check against concentrated conviction bets.
insights.quote: diversification is an act of humility. We don't know.

insights.claim: Of CPP's roughly $800 billion in assets, about $550 billion (roughly 70%) is cumulative investment income, up from a first contribution check of just $12 million about 27 years ago.
insights.speaker: guest, John Graham
insights.so_what: Concrete evidence of long-horizon compounding scale; useful benchmark for the contribution of investment returns versus inflows in a large pension.
insights.quote: of that 800 billion 550 billion is investment income

insights.claim: Graham remains constructive on private equity, calling it one of the biggest drivers of CPP's returns over the past 10-15 years despite acknowledging returns over the last couple of years have not met expectations.
insights.speaker: guest, John Graham
insights.so_what: A large LP publicly reaffirming PE commitment through the recent slump signals continued capital support for the asset class despite the drawdown in returns and distributions.
insights.quote: if I look over 10 15 years, it's it's been a big driver of value for CPP Investments

insights.claim: Graham defends the private governance model, arguing private ownership makes sense for certain companies at certain life-cycle stages by escaping public-market scrutiny and enabling actively engaged board-level investor governance.
insights.speaker: guest, John Graham
insights.so_what: Articulates the enduring rationale for take-privates; relevant to where PE can still add value beyond financial engineering.

insights.claim: CPP uses a partnership model in private markets, backing top external managers and then co-investing and co-underwriting alongside them at advantageous economics that avoid paying full fees and carry.
insights.speaker: guest, John Graham
insights.so_what: Shows how large LPs lower blended fee load via co-invest; internal and external returns are not directly comparable because internal teams ride external origination.
insights.quote: your internal teams are benefiting from the origination and the asset management of the external teams

insights.claim: Graham argues internal and external private-market teams should not be viewed as competing strategies but blended together, since internal co-investments are built off external managers' origination and asset management.
insights.speaker: guest, John Graham
insights.so_what: Reframes the insource-vs-outsource debate; the right performance metric is blended return, not internal beating external.

insights.claim: CPP holds roughly 45% of its portfolio in the US (ranging to about 50%) and actively manages against this band, with Graham noting that since the US is 65-70% of global equity market cap there is an argument the fund is actually underweight the US.
insights.speaker: guest, John Graham
insights.so_what: A large pension capping US exposure near 45-50% while calling itself underweight highlights the tension between home-bias limits and cap-weighted reality; watch for rebalancing pressure as US keeps outgrowing the book.
insights.quote: if if we don't manage it, it'll just keep growing and growing and growing as part of the portfolio

insights.claim: CPP has about 12% of its portfolio in Canada, and Graham says Canada looks more interesting than it has in years due to provincial and federal ambition to build big, cash-generative infrastructure that pension funds favor.
insights.speaker: guest, John Graham
insights.so_what: Signals rising Canadian infrastructure deal flow that could attract pension capital; a watch item for domestic real-asset opportunities.
insights.quote: Canada's looking more interesting than it has in in years

insights.claim: Graham advocates for competition for capital, saying he does not expect and does not want CPP to receive preferential domestic deals, because competitive capital drives the cost of capital down and produces better investment opportunities.
insights.speaker: guest, John Graham
insights.so_what: Counters political pressure for pensions to get sweetheart domestic deals; frames the US as the world's most competitive capital market and therefore its best-returning.
insights.quote: Competitive capital is a good thing.

insights.claim: CPP's China exposure has declined over recent years mainly because the rest of the portfolio grew around it rather than through active selling, and Graham argues a true long-term investor must retain some allocation to and deep knowledge of the world's second-largest economy.
insights.speaker: guest, John Graham
insights.so_what: Signals CPP is holding, not exiting, China but sizing carefully and avoiding sensitive sectors; a measured institutional stance amid broad Western derisking.
insights.quote: if you want to be a long-term investor, you have you have to have some level of investment in the world's second largest economy

insights.claim: In China, Graham says CPP must be very sensitive about which industries it enters, explicitly flagging defense and dual-use technologies as areas to avoid.
insights.speaker: guest, John Graham
insights.so_what: Defines the sector no-go lines for institutional China investing; relevant to geopolitical screening of any China-linked positions.
insights.quote: some areas like obviously be very sensitive to get into defense or to get into dual-use technologies

insights.claim: CPP Investments follows a policy of no blanket divestment from any industry, continuing to invest in and support oil and gas rather than exiting on principle.
insights.speaker: guest, John Graham
insights.so_what: A major pension explicitly refusing sector-wide fossil divestment signals continued institutional capital availability for energy; contrasts with peers pursuing exclusion.
insights.quote: we do not follow a path of blanket divestment

insights.claim: CPP has never directly invested in crypto as a deliberate choice, a stance the host jokingly notes has been unhelpful given recent crypto performance.
insights.speaker: guest, John Graham
insights.so_what: Confirms a large pension remains entirely on the sidelines of direct crypto exposure; a marker of continued institutional caution at this tier.
insights.quote: one thing we've never done is crypto. We've never directly invested in crypto.

insights.claim: CPP has exposure to roughly 50 countries but is only really active in about 12, deliberately concentrating expertise rather than trying to be an expert everywhere.
insights.speaker: guest, John Graham
insights.so_what: Shows how a global allocator bounds its opportunity set by expertise; concentration of active effort, not breadth, drives edge.
insights.quote: we're probably only really active in 12 because we just can't really be experts in every country

insights.claim: CPP's mandate, enshrined in federal legislation, is to maximize return without undue risk of loss accounting for factors that impact plan funding, with no government-set allocation to stocks or bonds, unlike Norway's ministry-set mandate.
insights.speaker: guest, John Graham
insights.so_what: Explains the governance source of CPP's allocation freedom; the independence-plus-accountability structure is the core of the exportable Canadian model.
insights.quote: Literally our mandate is is maximize return without undue risk of loss taking into account the factors that impact the plan

insights.claim: CPP Investments has fewer employees than three years ago (roughly flat) while managing about $300 billion more in assets, which Graham attributes partly to AI and process optimization creating operational leverage.
insights.speaker: guest, John Graham
insights.so_what: Concrete evidence that AI plus process work lets asset managers scale AUM without headcount; a template for cost-efficiency in the sector.
insights.quote: we have fewer employees than we did 3 years ago... but we have about 300 billion more of assets

insights.claim: Graham says AI has made CPP's decisions faster but whether it makes better investment decisions is still to be determined, and he remains cautious that AI will improve decision quality.
insights.speaker: guest, John Graham
insights.so_what: Tempers AI-alpha hype from a major institutional user; speed gains are real but decision-quality improvement is unproven, relevant for pricing AI's impact on active management.
insights.quote: It'll help us make faster decisions, yes. Will it help us make better decisions? TBD.

insights.claim: CPP has rolled out multiple large language models to every employee with training, courses, and bootcamps, reporting strong grassroots adoption while embedding AI in about half a dozen operational processes.
insights.speaker: guest, John Graham
insights.so_what: Illustrates the enterprise AI adoption path at a major financial institution; literacy-first deployment across the whole workforce.
insights.quote: we have rolled out multiple LLMs to every single employee in the organization

insights.claim: Graham calls it madness to stop hiring junior staff because of AI, insisting juniors are the future leaders of the organization, and expects AI to let CPP add hundreds of billions in assets without materially adding people.
insights.speaker: guest, John Graham
insights.so_what: Counters the narrative that AI will gut junior hiring in finance; the expected effect is scaling AUM per employee, not shrinking the workforce.
insights.quote: I'm not in the place of we we should stop hiring juniors. I think it's madness to stop hiring juniors

insights.claim: Graham, a former experimental scientist, argues investing is not science but a quantitative art requiring judgment and experience, because unlike replicable experiments the market is a living ecosystem changing every day and models are only a best guess about an uncertain future.
insights.speaker: guest, John Graham
insights.so_what: A caution against over-reliance on sophisticated quant models; underpins his skepticism that AI will improve decision quality.
insights.quote: It's a little bit of art and science. It's a It's a quantitative art.

insights.claim: Graham's key investing lesson is that you cannot diligence a bad investment into a good one, warning that extra work often just convinces you a fundamentally bad deal is good and that knowing when to quit is essential.
insights.speaker: guest, John Graham
insights.so_what: A discipline heuristic against sunk-cost and confirmation bias in deal underwriting; more diligence does not fix bad fundamentals.
insights.quote: you you can't diligence a bad investment into a good investment

insights.claim: Graham says any investor who claims never to have been humbled is either not taking much risk or not being truthful, framing failures as inherent to genuine risk-taking.
insights.speaker: guest, John Graham
insights.so_what: A candor marker on institutional risk-taking; useful cultural signal for evaluating manager honesty about drawdowns.
insights.quote: any investor who says they haven't been humbled um is is probably not either taking a lot of risk or is is not being overly uh truthful

insights.claim: Graham insists CPP cannot have a star culture and must build investment programs around organizational strengths rather than individuals, because as a multi-generational institution meant to endure 50-75 years it is the opposite of a founder-led firm.
insights.speaker: guest, John Graham
insights.so_what: Explains why CPP institutionalizes process over star managers; a durability principle distinguishing enduring institutions from personality-driven funds.
insights.quote: what we can't have at CPP Investments is a star culture

insights.claim: Graham argues decisions should be made by the people closest to the information via delegation into geographies and asset classes, but stresses that delegation without alignment leads to chaos.
insights.speaker: guest, John Graham
insights.so_what: A governance principle for scaling decision-making in large allocators; decentralization only works paired with shared mandate alignment.
insights.quote: Delegation without alignment leads to chaos.

insights.claim: Graham says leaders almost always wait too long to assemble the right senior team, advising new CEOs to be able to say by their one-year mark that their senior team is exactly who they want.
insights.speaker: guest, John Graham
insights.so_what: A leadership timing lesson applicable to evaluating new management teams; slowness to reshape the top team is a common regret.
insights.quote: when that one year anniversary hits, you should be able to look around your senior team and say this is exactly who I want

insights.claim: Graham describes bureaucracy as one-directional accumulation that never self-reduces, requiring periodic deliberate purges every few years to declutter processes.
insights.speaker: guest, John Graham
insights.so_what: An organizational-efficiency heuristic; institutions need forced periodic simplification because complexity only compounds otherwise.
insights.quote: you will not naturally, you know, de-bureaucratize

insights.claim: CPP Investments discloses nearly every investment it makes in quarterly statements, going beyond what the CPPIB Act requires, on the view that Canadians should understand how their public-good savings are invested.
insights.speaker: guest, John Graham
insights.so_what: High transparency makes CPP's positioning observable to the market; its quarterly disclosures are a usable signal for tracking large-institution deal flow.
insights.quote: within our quarterly statements, we release pretty much every investment we make
